explanation: 'prop_0 until prop_1 but no later than prop_2' means that prop_0 should hold up until prop_1 happens, and prop_1 must happen before or at the same time as prop_2 (prop_1 must happen not later than prop_2). 'After that the prop_3' means prop_3 holds after the point where either prop_1 or prop_2 occurs (i.e., whichever happens first).

The phrase means that 'prop_0' holds up to 'prop_1', but 'prop_1' must happen before 'prop_2', and after 'prop_1' happens, 'prop_3' holds. In LTL, 'prop_0 until prop_1' is 'prop_0 U prop_1'. The requirement that 'prop_1' happens no later than 'prop_2' is 'F prop_1 -> F[0:1] prop_1 | G (!prop_2)' (but we can say 'F (prop_2 & !prop_1) -> G (!prop_1)' if 'prop_2' happens before 'prop_1', then 'prop_1' never happens). The behavior after 'prop_1' is that 'prop_3' should hold globally. Putting it together, we have: (1) 'prop_0 U (prop_1 | prop_2)', (2) if 'prop_2' happens before 'prop_1', then 'prop_1' never happens, (3) after 'prop_1', 'G prop_3'. Combined: '((prop_0 U prop_1) & ((prop_2 U prop_1) | G!prop_2)) & G(prop_1 -> G prop_3)'. To make it fully proper, we ensure that after 'prop_1', 'prop_3' holds, and 'prop_1' must happen no later than 'prop_2'.

The requirement describes that 'prop_0' holds until 'prop_1' happens, but 'prop_1' must occur before or at the same time as 'prop_2'. After either 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' happens, 'prop_3' must hold. This is translated by ensuring that until ('prop_1' | 'prop_2'), 'prop_0' holds, and if 'prop_1' happens before 'prop_2', then 'prop_3' must hold after. If 'prop_2' happens first, then also transition to 'prop_3'.

The statement requires that 'prop_0' holds until 'prop_1', but 'prop_1' must happen before or at the same time as 'prop_2'. After 'prop_1' occurs (and before 'prop_2'), 'prop_3' must hold thereafter. We express 'prop_0 until prop_1', and also that 'prop_1' occurs no later than 'prop_2' (i.e., 'prop_2' cannot happen before 'prop_1'). Finally, after 'prop_1', 'prop_3' always holds.

The formula captures that 'prop_0' holds up until 'prop_1', but both this and 'prop_1' must happen before or at the same time as 'prop_2'. After 'prop_1' occurs (and before 'prop_2'), 'prop_3' must hold globally thereafter.

The formula encodes that 'prop_0' must hold until 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' holds, but not after 'prop_2' first occurs (no later than 'prop_2'). If 'prop_2' happens before 'prop_1', 'prop_0' must continue to hold up to 'prop_2'. After either 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' (whichever comes first), 'prop_3' must hold globally.

This specification requires that prop_0 holds continuously until prop_1 becomes true, but prop_1 must occur no later than the occurrence of prop_2 (i.e., prop_1 must happen before or at the same time as prop_2 appears, and not after prop_2). After prop_1 occurs (and not after prop_2), prop_3 must hold globally. We encode the 'no later than' restriction by ensuring that if prop_2 occurs and prop_1 has not yet occurred, then prop_1 must never occur afterward. Then, after prop_1, prop_3 holds globally.
output_LTL: ((inspiratoryPhase) U ((P_insp_greaterThanOrEqual_MaxP_insp) | (inspClock_greaterThanOrEqual_inspiratoryTime))) & G( ( !(P_insp_greaterThanOrEqual_MaxP_insp) & (inspClock_greaterThanOrEqual_inspiratoryTime) ) -> G(!(P_insp_greaterThanOrEqual_MaxP_insp)) ) & G( ((P_insp_greaterThanOrEqual_MaxP_insp) & !((inspClock_greaterThanOrEqual_inspiratoryTime) U (P_insp_greaterThanOrEqual_MaxP_insp))) -> G (expiratoryPhaseStart) )

explanation: The inspiration phase is being described as lasting ('active') until either prop_1 occurs, but not beyond the occurrence of prop_2. This is modeled using 'U' (until), but we must ensure that the phase does not outlast prop_2. That's achieved by ending if either prop_1 or prop_2 happens, so if prop_2 happens, the phase must stop even if prop_1 does not. After this phase ends (when prop_1 or prop_2 happens), prop_3 must hold next. This leads to a conjunction of two parts: (1) The phase holds until (prop_1 or prop_2), and (2) globally, if (prop_1 or prop_2) holds, then in the next step prop_3 holds.

Since 'insp' cannot be used and only the given atomic propositions are allowed, we must model the semantics entirely with 'prop_1', 'prop_2', and 'prop_3'. The inspiration phase 'lasts until prop_1 but no later than prop_2', which means the inspiration phase ends when either prop_1 or prop_2 happens (whichever occurs first). After the first occurrence of (prop_1 | prop_2), prop_3 must hold. We express 'after that, prop_3' as: whenever (prop_1 | prop_2) becomes true, in the next state prop_3 holds. This is done globally for every occurrence of (prop_1 | prop_2).

'prop_0 until prop_1 but no later than prop_2' means prop_0 must hold at all times before (and including) the occurrence of prop_1, and prop_1 must happen before or at the same time as prop_2—i.e., we never pass prop_2 without prop_1 happening. Then, after prop_1 or prop_2 have happened, prop_3 must hold from then on. This is captured in LTL by enforcing ((prop_0 U (prop_1 | prop_2)) & G (prop_2 -> !prop_1 U prop_2)) for the first statement, and G((prop_1 | prop_2) -> G(prop_3)) for the second statement. Combined: ((prop_0 U (prop_1 | prop_2)) & G (prop_2 -> !prop_1 U prop_2)) & G((prop_1 | prop_2) -> G(prop_3))
